claimed_summary: GPIO Lock Register bypass via REG_PADOUTSET/REG_PADOUTCLR: The GPIO lock mechanism (r_gpio_lock) blocks writes to REG_PADDIR and REG_PADOUT, but REG_PADOUTSET and REG_PADOUTCLR can modify GPIO outputs without checking the lock bits, allowing unauthorized GPIO output manipulation.
claimed_signals: r_gpio_lock, pwdata_l, r_gpio_out, r_gpio_dir; r_gpio_lock; REG_PADDIR / REG_PADOUT write logic with lock check; REG_PADOUTSET and REG_PADOUTCLR definitions
scorer_notes: PADOUTSET/PADOUTCLR lock bypass is not a visible GT case for this scope.

claimed_summary: GPIO Lock Register (REG_GPIOLOCK) is freely rewritable with no write-once or escalate-only protection, allowing an attacker to clear the lock after it has been set.
claimed_signals: r_gpio_lock; r_gpio_lock reset initialization; r_gpio_lock write; Lock check logic
scorer_notes: Correctly identifies REG_GPIOLOCK/r_gpio_lock writable through APB, defeating the lock mechanism.

claimed_summary: APB bus node (apb_node) has no access control or privilege filtering: any APB master can access any peripheral based solely on address matching, with no master identity or permission checks.
claimed_signals: psel_o; apb_masters; Address decode and psel generation; Module interface; periph_bus_defines.sv
scorer_notes: Generic APB no-access-control claim does not match address-overlap GT cases without overlap interval evidence.

claimed_summary: GPIO Lock Register (GPIOLOCK) is not self-protecting — any bus master can unlock GPIO configuration by writing to the lock register, defeating the permission/lock mechanism entirely.

scorer_notes: Correctly identifies GPIOLOCK as writable/self-unprotected through APB.

claimed_summary: PADOUTSET and PADOUTCLR registers may bypass the GPIO output lock (r_gpio_lock[2]) — allowing modification of GPIO output state even when locked.

scorer_notes: PADOUTSET/PADOUTCLR lock bypass is not a visible GT case for this scope.
